coredata.dc:title: N-doped carbon dots incorporated chitosan/polyvinylpyrrolidone based polymer film for advanced packaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Kalanidhi K,
Nagaraaj P,
coredata.dc:description: We have explored the use of N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) as a strengthening agent for the preparation of advanced packaging materials. The incorporation of NCDs in chitosan-polyvinylpyrrolidone (chitosan-PVP) film was achieved by a simple solution casting method to improve the mechanical properties and biodegradability of the film. Orange peel extract has flavouring and antimicrobial properties, incorporated into the film. The synthesized films showed enhanced tensile strength and elongation at break and moisture resistance properties. Moreover, it showed excellent biodegradation ability and promotes plant growth. Therefore, NCDs incorporated film with orange peel extract can be recommended for food packaging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon dots
Polymeric composite film
Orange peel extract
Chitosan-Polyvinylpyrrolidone
Packaging materials

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Moisture content and solubility analysis of films
Effect of film degraded soil on seed germination and growth of plant

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Polymer film of (a) Chitosan-PVP (b) NCDs-Chitosan-PVP (c) Orange peel-Chitosan-PVP (d) NCDs-Orange peel/chitosan-PVP.
SEM images of synthesized bio films (a) Chitosan-PVP (b) Orange peel/chitosan-PVP (c) NCDs/Chitosan-PVP (d) NCDs and orange peel/chitosan-PVP (e) Orange peel extract (f) TEM image of NCDs.

FT-IR spectra of synthesized films; (a) Chitosan/PVP film (b) Carbon dots incorporated Chitosan/PVP film (c) CDs and orange peel extract incorporated Chitosan/PVP film (d) Orange peel extract (e) CDs.
(a) Tensile strength of synthesized films (b) Elongation at break of synthesized films.
Contact angle measurement for synthesized films.
Photographic image of biodegradability study of all synthesized films.
Photographic images of black gram plant growth on film degraded soil.
Moisture content and solubility analysis of the film.
Effect of film degraded soil on seed germination and growth of plant.

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon dots incorporated chitosan/polyvinylpyrrolidone based polymer film for advanced packaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Anna University, Chennai 600025, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Anna University, Chennai 600025, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We have explored the use of N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) as a strengthening agent for the preparation of advanced packaging materials. The incorporation of NCDs in chitosan-polyvinylpyrrolidone (chitosan-PVP) film was achieved by a simple solution casting method to improve the mechanical properties and biodegradability of the film. Orange peel extract has flavouring and antimicrobial properties, incorporated into the film. The synthesized films showed enhanced tensile strength and elongation at break and moisture resistance properties. Moreover, it showed excellent biodegradation ability and promotes plant growth. Therefore, NCDs incorporated film with orange peel extract can be recommended for food packaging applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All the data that support the findings of this work are included within the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The demand for environmentally benign and biodegradable packaging materials has increased over synthetic materials due to positive impacts on food materials. The problems concerned with the usage of synthetic materials are proper disposal and biodegradability which lead to serious environmental issues [1–3]. Biopolymer based packaging materials have been gaining attraction owing to their non-toxicity, biodegradability and eco-friendliness and are considered a potential alternative to currently using synthetic materials. Chitosan is a water insoluble bio-polymer, it has been explored widely as a packing material due to its biodegradability, environmental concern, antimicrobial activity and good film-forming quality. Moreover, the wide range of applications has been limited due to the poor mechanical and barrier properties [4–8]. The enhancement of these properties makes chitosan to higher-end applications. For that, further modification is required in order to enhance the mechanical properties of chitosan based film in packaging applications. There are different kinds of materials have been employed to enhance the properties of the film. Earlier reports mainly focus on the combination of chitosan and graphene oxide has been used for film preparation with enhanced mechanical and thermal properties [9]. Another study used a combination of chitosan and ZnO for film preparation and showed improved mechanical and antimicrobial properties [10].

Carbon based nanomaterials have created increased attention due to their excellent electrical, optical, thermal, and mechanical properties [11,12]. It has been used as a nanofiller in polymer to obtain superior properties than pristine polymer [13]. Carbon dots (CDs), a new class of emerging carbon based nanomaterial have gained importance due to their excellent physicochemical properties such as chemical inertness, stability, photoluminescence property, abundant, non-toxicity, low cost, and biocompatible [14–18]. These interesting properties make CDs a potential candidate in various applications such as bio-imaging [16], sensors [17,19], super capacitor [20], catalysis [21] and corrosion inhibitor [22–24]. CDs derived from natural resources and its incorporation into chitosan-PVP film has gained increased attention in packaging applications.

In this research work, naturally derived NCDs were incorporated into chitosan-PVP film by a simple solution casting method along with orange peel extract as a novel combination. Here, chitosan- polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) (chitosan-PVP) is the base polymer composition in which NCDs and orange peel are added and their properties were studied. PVP is a non-toxic, transparent, food grade polymeric material. It has excellent film forming properties [25]. The orange peel extract is a natural flavoring agent and has excellent antimicrobial properties due to the presence of the phenolic compounds which easily penetrate through the bacterial cell membrane and arrest the cellular metabolism and DNA synthesis as a result of the killing of bacterial cell [26–28]. Later, the prepared films were characterized by FTIR, SEM, mechanical property, moisture content, solubility, water contact angle, biodegradability analysis and plant growth. The obtained film has been compared with chitosan- PVP and their properties are discussed in detail.
Preparation procedure of NCDs was followed from our previous report [29] (Experimental details can be given in supporting information).

The biopolymer film was prepared by simple solution casting method as reported by with some modifications [30]. Chitosan based biopolymer film was prepared with four different compositions such as chitosan-PVP, chitosan-PVP/orange peel extract, chitosan-PVP/NCDs and chitosan-PVP/NCDs/orange peel extract. The chitosan based film was prepared by adding 1 wt% chitosan in 2% (v/v) acetic acid solution. Then the solution was placed under magnetic stirring at (300–600) rpm for 2–3 h to get a complete dissolution. After complete mixing, 0.1 wt% of PVP was added to above solution and kept under stirring, followed by 30 µl of glutaraldehyde (crosslinker) was added to the solution and mixed thoroughly. Then, the obtained solution was poured in to the petri dishes and dried for 24 h at 60 °C. The chitosan-PVP film was peeled off from the petri dishes and stored at room temperature for further use.

The above mentioned combination of biopolymer was prepared by mixing 2 wt% chitosan, 0.1 wt% PVP and 1 mL of NCDs with 2 mL of orange peel extract. The biopolymer combinations such as chitosan-PVP/orange peel extract, chitosan-PVP/NCDs and chitosan-PVP-NCDs-orange peel extract solution were poured into the petri dishes and dried at 60 °C for 24 h. The four set of films was prepared (Fig. 1 ) and peeled off from the petri dishes and stored at a temperature of 25 °C and used for further characterization and use. The average thickness of chitosan-PVP, orange peel incorporated chitosan-PVP, NCDs incorporated chitosan-PVP and Orange peel/ NCDs/chitosan-PVP was found to be 0.055 mm, 0.069 mm, 0.052 mm and 0.070 mm for respectively.

The tensile strength and elongation at break were measured using Universal Testing Machine (Model H50 K-S Hounsfield Test Equipment Ltd, UK). The rectangular shaped film was obtained by cutting the film with the dimension of 2.5x7 cm which is applied to tensile and elongation measurements.

The moisture content of the synthesized films was determined as reported in the previous literature with some modifications [31]. The initial weight of the film was taken and it was kept at 80 °C for 12 h. Later, the weight of the film after the treatment was taken in that moisture content of film has been eliminated. Then, the moisture content of the film was calculated using the following Eq. (1).
(1)
M o i s t u r e
C o n t e n t
( % )
=
(
W i
-
W d
/
W i
)
∗
100
(Wi – Initial weight before drying, Wd- Final Weight after drying)

The water solubility test was adopted from the previous report with some modifications [32]. The initial weight of film was taken and it was placed in a beaker containing water and ensures the film was completely soaked in water. It was allowed to stand for 12 h to study the solubility nature of the film. Later, it was taken out and dried at 100 °C for 12 h. Then the weight of the film was taken. The water solubility of the film was determined from equation (2).
(2)
W a t e r
s o l u b i l i t y
( % )
=
(
W d
, I n i t i a l -
W d
, F i n a l /
W d
, I n i t i a l )
∗
100
(Wd, Initial, Wd, Final are initial and final dry weight of the film samples)

The biodegradability study of all synthesized films was examined by the soil burial method as mentioned in the literature [33,34]. Garden soil was taken and filled with a plastic container that has small holes for aeration and water circulation in which weighed film samples were placed such a way it should be completely covered with soil. The water was poured to moist the soil and the whole study was examined under normal atmospheric conditions. Later, it was monitored for 3 weeks and the weight of the samples has been taken at regular intervals of time by measuring the weight loss through an electronic weighing balance from which the percentage of weight loss was calculated for the synthesized films from Eq. (3).
(3)
% w e i g h t
l o s s = ( I n i t i a l
w e i g h t
a t
t h e
b e g i n n i n g - F i n a l
w e i g h t
a f t e r
3
w e e k s ) / I n i t i a l
w e i g h t
a t
t h e
b e g i n n i n g )
∗
100

It is important to study whether the degraded polymeric film has any harmful effect on plant growth. For that, the pot containing different films (Chitosan/PVP, NCDs/Chitosan/PVP, Chitosan/PVP/orange peel, Chitosan/PVP/NCDs/orange peel) including blank (without any film) for degradation was taken in which seeds were kept uniformly. Later, the pot was allowed to wet at regular intervals and it was monitored for a week in an open atmosphere. Later, seed germination and plant growth were examined [35].

The surface morphology of prepared films was analysed through Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and displayed in Fig. 1. Chitosan-PVP film (a) shows a smooth surface as that of chitosan film [30]. But it retains the same smoothness even with the incorporation of PVP [36]. (b) Orange peel incorporated film showed a smooth and layered structure (c) NCDs incorporated film reveals a smooth surface where NCDs were well distributed in chitosan-PVP film whereas, (d) NCDs incorporated chitosan-PVP-orange peel film showed a smooth and uniform surface as observed in the chitosan-PVP film. In addition, the SEM image of orange peel extract was displayed in Fig. 2 (e). It shows the uniformly distributed particle with particle agglomeration. Fig. 2(f) displays the TEM image of NCDs which was shown a spherical in morphology and size of less than 10 nm which is reported in our previous work [18]. Therefore, according to the result obtained, incorporation of NCDs and orange peel extract in chitosan-PVP film has been completely distributed over the surface of the film and retaining the smooth surface.

Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis was performed to confirm the functional groups and study the interaction between compounds present in the film (Fig. 3 ). For chitosan-PVP film, the broad absorption band was observed in the range of 3300–3600 cm−1 due to stretching vibration of O-H and N-H whereas a peak at 2920 cm−1 was attributed to stretching vibration of aliphatic C-H bonds. The absorption peak at 1647 cm−1 was assigned to amide C = O stretching frequency, which indicates the presence of N-acetyl groups in chitosan. The absorption peak at 1570 cm−1 showed N-H bending of amide. The absorption bands at 1147 cm−1 and 1042 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of C-O-C and C-O, respectively. In the case of carbon dots incorporated Chitosan-PVP film, the broadening of an absorption peak at 3300–3600 cm−1 and intense as compared with chitosan-PVP. In addition, the intensity of 1647 cm−1absorption peak gets increased as not in the chitosan-PVP. These changes might be due to the strong hydrogen bond formed between surface functional groups of NCDs and active groups present in chitosan-PVP film and claims that the successful incorporation and interaction of carbon dots in the chitosan-PVP film [37]. Whereas, NCDs and orange peel incorporated film showed the similar spectral characteristics peaks as in the NCDs incorporated chitosan-PVP film. In addition, the intensity of 1653 cm−1 was slightly reduced due to incorporation of orange peel. The FTIR spectrum of orange peel extract prepared in aqueous solution and NCDs were showed a peak around 3300–3600 cm−1 and 1635 cm−1 due to the existence of OH/N-H and C = O functional groups in it. In all films, we noted the similar spectra with their characteristics peaks except for an increase and decrease in intensity of peak caused by carbon dots, orange peel extract which confirms the successful incorporation of carbon dots, orange peel extract in the chitosan-PVP film.

Fig. 4 shows the tensile strength and elongation at break of different kinds of chitosan-PVP based films. Fig. 4(a) displays the tensile strength of chitosan-PVP, Orange peel/chitosan-PVP, NCDs/chitosan-PVP, Orange peel/NCDs/Chitosan-PVP. The NCDs incorporated chitosan-PVP blend film showed a better tensile strength compared to other synthesized films due to the addition of NCDs as nanofiller. Whereas orange peel extract incorporated film shows slightly lesser tensile strength compared to other synthesized films. The reason for the higher tensile strength might be the strong interaction between NCDs and chitosan to form a hydrogen bonding, which helps to improve the tensile strength of the film. Generally, carbon based materials has been used as nanofiller to enhance the mechanical properties of the film as reported in the earlier study [9]. Similarly, incorporation of NCDs in the chitosan-PVP films helps to improve elongation at break of the film. Whereas, the orange peel extract and NCDs incorporated film shows a less elongation at break might be due to a higher crosslinking effect by the incorporation and interaction of NCDs and orange peel extract with chitosan-PVP. (Fig. 4(b)). Therefore, the result reveals the film has gained better tensile strength and elongation at break due to the inclusion of NCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Moisture content and solubility analysis of films
ce:section.ce:para: The moisture resistant capacity of film is an important parameter that needs to be studied which is the criterion of food grade quality of packaging film. Table 1 shows the moisture content of the synthesized films. The moisture content of NCDs incorporated film gets reduced as compared to chitosan-PVP film. Similarly, orange peel incorporated film also exhibited a reduction in moisture content. Whereas the moisture content of both NCDs and orange peel incorporated films also was decreased due to the addition of NCDs and orange peel extract. The reduction in moisture content of the film confirms the uniform distribution and interaction of NCDs and orange peel extract within the chitosan-PVP film. In this result, the moisture content and solubility showed an opposite trend as reported in the literature [1].

The water solubility analysis for the synthesized films was studied and displayed in Table 1. The chitosan/PVP film shows a water solubility of around 11% whereas NCDs incorporated film exhibits slight increase in water solubility compared to chitosan/PVP film and orange peel incorporated film shows the higher water solubility compare to all the synthesized films. NCDs and orange peel incorporated film also showed increased water solubility compared to chitosan-PVP film. The increment in water solubility compared to chitosan/PVP film indicates that film bears more polar functional groups due to the inclusion of NCDs and orange peel extract which allow the solubility of the films.

The wetting properties of film were studied by analysing the hydrophilic/hydrophobic nature of synthesized films by contact angle measurement which was displayed in Fig. 5 . Chitosan-PVP film exhibits a contact angle value of 90.5⁰. Whereas, orange peel incorporated chitosan-PVP film shows higher contact angle value than chitosan-PVP. NCDs incorporated chitosan-PVP film exhibits similar to that of chitosan-PVP film. Orange peel and NCDs incorporated films show a contact angle value of 89.6⁰. These results were obtained based on the film surface topology and the components present in the synthesized films

The degradability of synthesized films was studied for 50 days by soil burial method as depicted in Fig. 6 . The degradation of film is mainly explained by the hydrolysis process. The water content plays a crucial role in degrading the film. The water molecule present in the soil and surface of the materials can help to grow and multiply the microorganisms which involve in the degrading the polymer film. On other hand, it undergoes a hydrolysis process with water molecules in which cleavage of hydrogen bond occurs in the polymer backbone and is converted into smaller fragments. The existence of the hydrophilic nature of substances in the films can also accelerate the degradation process [34]. There are some other factors that are also paid attention towards degradation process such as light, temperature, air, pH, the quantity of microorganism and nature of the soil. Here, all synthesized films (Chitosan/PVP, Chitosan/PVP film inclusion with NCDs, Chitosan/PVP film inclusion with orange peel extract, Chitosan/PVP film inclusion with NCDs and orange peel extract) exhibited above 40% degradation for 50 days. The variation in the degradation percentage in each film depends on the composition and nature of the component present in the film. Moreover, NCDs and orange peel incorporated chitosan/PVP film exhibit higher degradation efficiency than chitosan/PVP alone. The photographic images of all the degraded films were displayed in Fig. 7 . These results conclude that the obtained film could be a good replacement tool for synthetic films and eco-friendly in nature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of film degraded soil on seed germination and growth of plant

ce:section.ce:para: In the present study, naturally derived NCDs were successfully incorporated into chitosan-PVP film along with orange peel extract as a flavoring agent by a simple solution casting method. The mechanical properties of NCDs incorporated film show an increase in tensile strength and elongation rate due to the presence of NCDs as a strengthening agent. The incorporation of orange peel extract in the film is a unique combination to improve the film properties. The biodegradability study and plant growth have been studied for the synthesized films. Overall, NCDs and Orange peel incorporated films showed improved properties compared to chitosan/PVP. Hence, NCDs and orange peel extract incorporated chitosan-PVP film could be a potential alternative for currently exiting synthetic plastic film. The antimicrobial activity of the synthesized films will be studied in the future.
Kalanidhi K: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Nagaraaj P: Visualization, Supervision, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author thanks to University Grants Commission, New Delhi, India (IN) for providing NFOBC fellowship (NFO-2018-19-OBC-TAM-79393) and the authors also thank to Department of Chemistry, Anna University for constant support and encouragement.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cplett.2022.139960.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

EC Microbiol.
